title: Lipstick on White Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (wax + oil + pigment/dye). Lipstick deposits three layers: waxy structure (carnauba, beeswax), oil carrier (castor oil, lanolin, mineral oil), and pigment — typically iron oxide for reds/pinks plus synthetic D&C dyes. On white cotton, this is one of the most treatable lipstick combinations because cotton tolerates aggressive chemistry (pH 6-11, heat to 95°C) and white gives full bleaching latitude. Dan Eisen's lipstick procedure: petroleum jelly to loosen pigments, detergent and water, ammonia, then hydrogen peroxide and ammonia, then vinegar. The sequence matters — scrape wax, dissolve oil, then attack the dye.
whyThisWorks: White cotton gives us the full arsenal: mechanical wax removal → POG for oil → commercial ink remover for synthetic dye → oxidizing bleach for iron oxide pigment → reducing bleach as final escalation. Each step addresses one phase and reveals the next. Dan Eisen notes that commercial ink removers are effective on lipstick because they target the same class of synthetic dyes. On white cotton, we can steam, bleach, and flush aggressively — this fiber forgives aggressive treatment.

spottingProtocol.technique: Scrape surface wax — Dan Eisen method
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape off as much solid lipstick as possible with a spatula or credit card edge. Dan Eisen recommends applying petroleum jelly (Vaseline) as a lubricant — put a small amount on the stain and gently work with the back of a spoon to loosen and lift the waxy pigment. Use a towel to blot up loosened material. This mechanical step removes 40-60% of the stain before any chemistry is applied. On cotton, you can be moderately aggressive with scraping — the fiber is strong.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull spatula, petroleum jelly, clean white towels, spoon

spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve the oil/wax carrier — dryside spotting
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG agent liberally to the remaining stain. Work on the spotting board with firm tamping action — cotton handles mechanical action well. The POG dissolves remaining wax and oil carrier, releasing trapped pigment. You should see significant color transfer to the towel underneath. Flush with VDS. Repeat until oil transfer stops. Dan Eisen's dryside sequence for cosmetics: POG first, complete dryside before moving to wetside.
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, spotting brush, VDS, clean white towels

spottingProtocol.technique: Detergent flush followed by ammonia treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Per Dan Eisen's lipstick procedure: apply water and detergent (NSD), use spoon or brush to loosen remaining stain, blot with towel. Then apply dilute ammonia (28% ammonia diluted — cotton handles ammonia well at pH 11). The ammonia helps break down the protein-based components of lipstick (some formulas contain collagen or silk amino acids as moisturizers). Work area, rinse with water. On white cotton, ammonia is fully safe.
spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, ammonia 28%, water gun, spotting brush

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidizing bleach — white cotton advantage for pigment removal
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dan Eisen's next step: apply hydrogen peroxide 6% and ammonia. On white cotton, apply steam — heat accelerates the bleaching reaction. The peroxide breaks the chromophore of synthetic D&C dyes. For iron oxide pigment (red/pink lipstick), peroxide is less effective — iron oxide is a mineral, not an organic dye. If red pigment persists after peroxide, this is likely iron oxide. Oxalic acid (diluted, 5 minutes max, rinse thoroughly) can chelate the iron. On white cotton, oxalic acid is rated Grade B — safe with proper application and neutralization.
spottingProtocol.equipment: H2O2 6%, ammonia 28%, steam gun, oxalic acid (if needed for iron oxide)

spottingProtocol.instruction: Dan Eisen's final step: apply vinegar (acetic acid) and wash. On the spotting board, flush with dilute acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize ammonia residue. Follow with thorough water flush. On cellulose fibers, per sequence rules: neutralize acid residue to prevent continued cellulose degradation. Air dry and inspect.

Air dry. Inspect under strong light. On white cotton, any residual pink/red pigment will be clearly visible against the white background. If shadow remains: try reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) — per Eisen, reducing bleach is specifically effective on the last traces of lipstick. Dissolve in warm water, apply with Q-tip, rinse within 15 minutes. Once clean, cotton handles standard press temperature.

homeSolutions: Scrape off solid lipstick with a credit card. Apply petroleum jelly to the remaining mark — work with the back of a spoon, blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the cloth shows minimal color transfer.
Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work with an old toothbrush using gentle circular motions. Rinse with warm water. The surfactant emulsifies the remaining oil phase.
For remaining color: apply 3% hydrogen peroxide directly to the stain. Let sit 5-10 minutes on white cotton — this is safe. Blot. The peroxide breaks down the synthetic dye.

Dan Eisen's batch method for a lipstick-contaminated laundry load: fill washer with warm water, add 4 oz citrus-based nonionic detergent (Simple Green) per gallon. Agitate 30 minutes with no rinse cycle. Then re-wash normally. Inspect before drying.
If pink stain persists after peroxide: rinse with cool water, apply white vinegar, let sit 5 minutes, re-wash. The acid helps break iron oxide pigment.
materialWarnings: NEVER use a dryer until lipstick is confirmed gone — heat permanently sets both synthetic dye and iron oxide pigment into cotton.
Dan Eisen: if lipstick transferred to an entire load during washing, use the batch run method rather than individual spotting — it's more efficient.
Chlorine bleach may be tempting on white cotton but can actually SET iron oxide — turning it darker. Use hydrogen peroxide first.
Scrape wax BEFORE applying any liquid — liquid spreads wax across a larger area.
Test all chemicals on a hidden seam even on white cotton — some cottons have optical brighteners that react unpredictably.
escalation: If lipstick persists after full protocol: the residual is likely iron oxide pigment. Apply oxalic acid paste (5% solution, 5 minutes max, rinse thoroughly, neutralize with baking soda solution). On white cotton, oxalic acid is safe with proper technique. If that fails, commercial rust remover may address the iron oxide component. Per the product guide: A.L. Wilson Picrin is the nuclear option for iron oxide — but it's HF-based and requires full PPE and safety protocol. For a lipstick stain, Picrin is rarely necessary unless the garment has been heat-set with the stain.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover

professional.note: Dissolves wax and oil carrier in lipstick formula.

professional.name: Chemspec Ink-Go
professional.note: Eisen notes commercial ink removers are effective on lipstick dyes.

professional.name: Sodium Hydrosulfite (Reducing Bleach)
professional.note: Per Eisen: specifically effective on last traces of lipstick. Q-tip application.

household.name: Petroleum Jelly (Vaseline)
household.note: Eisen's recommended lubricant for lipstick wax and pigment removal.

household.name: Dawn Dish Soap (Original Blue)
household.note: Effective oil-cutting surfactant for the lipstick oil phase.

household.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
household.note: Safe on white cotton. Breaks synthetic dye chromophore.

customerExplanation: Lipstick is three stains in one: wax, oil, and pigment. We treat each layer in order — scrape the wax, dissolve the oil with professional solvents, then use bleaching chemistry to remove the color. On white cotton, we have the advantage of being able to use professional-strength bleaching that safely targets the pigment without risking the fabric.
commonMistakes: Rubbing the lipstick smear before scraping — this embeds wax deeper and spreads pigment. Always scrape first. | Applying water before solvent — water locks the oil carrier into the cotton weave. Start dryside. | Using the dryer before the stain is gone — heat sets lipstick pigment permanently. | Not recognizing iron oxide pigment — if red color persists after peroxide, it's mineral pigment, not organic dye, and needs acid treatment not oxidative bleach.
scienceNote: Lipstick is the textbook combination stain: wax (nonpolar solid), oil (nonpolar liquid), and pigment (either mineral or organic dye). Dan Eisen classified it alongside ink as a combination dryside/wetside stain. The reducing bleach connection is important: Eisen specifically lists lipstick alongside ink, hair dye, and fruit as stains where reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) removes the last traces. This is because the D&C dyes used in lipstick have similar chromophore structures to those in inks — triarylmethane and azo compounds that respond to reductive chromophore cleavage.
callPro: Fresh lipstick on white cotton has a high success rate with home methods. For set lipstick (especially if heat-exposed), professional treatment provides access to the full chemistry escalation and spotting board technique.
difficulty_factors: White cotton gives full bleaching latitude — high success rate
Three-phase stain but each phase responds well to standard chemistry
Cotton tolerates aggressive mechanical action and chemistry
solventNote: For PERC shops: pre-spot on board, then machine process. If lipstick transferred to an entire load, use Dan Eisen's batch run method: low water level, citrus-based nonionic detergent, 30-minute agitation. For wet clean: launder after spotting with warm water and enzyme detergent.
